expectations.text: Clearly states that ISO 27001 certification does NOT equal NIS2 compliance (a strong foundation but not a safe harbor — legal obligations remain directly on the entity)
expectations.evidence: 'Short answer: No. ISO 27001:2022 certification is strong evidence of a mature information security management system (ISMS)... but it does not by itself satisfy NIS2.'

expectations.text: Identifies the incident-reporting gap: NIS2 Art. 23 prescribes 24h / 72h / 1-month reporting timelines to CSIRTs/authorities that ISO 27001 does not require
expectations.evidence: 'Incident notification timelines and content. NIS2 mandates a strict, multi-stage incident reporting regime: early warning within 24 hours, incident notification within 72 hours, and a final report within one month... ISO 27001 (Annex A 5.24–5.28) requires an incident management process but has no equivalent fixed statutory deadlines or regulator-facing reporting format.'

expectations.text: Identifies the governance gap: Art. 20 management-body approval, oversight, mandatory training, and personal accountability go beyond ISO 27001's management commitment clauses
expectations.evidence: 'Management body accountability and liability. NIS2 explicitly requires management/board-level approval of cybersecurity risk-management measures, mandates cybersecurity training for management, and exposes management to personal liability and potential temporary bans from managerial roles for non-compliance. ISO 27001 requires top-management commitment (Clause 5) but does not carry the same statutory personal liability regime.'

expectations.text: Discusses supply chain security under Art. 21(2)(d) — and if it cites the EU-level coordinated supply chain risk assessments, attributes them to Art. 22 / the Cooperation Group with ENISA. FAIL this assertion if coordinated supply-chain risk assessments are attributed to Art. 26 (which concerns jurisdiction and territoriality).
expectations.evidence: 'Supply chain and third-party risk depth. NIS2 places explicit emphasis on supply chain security, including assessing the cybersecurity practices of direct suppliers/service providers and considering vulnerabilities specific to each supplier relationship (informed by ENISA coordinated risk assessments for critical sectors).' Supply chain security is discussed substantively, and the ENISA coordinated risk assessment mention carries no article citation at all — it is not misattributed to Art. 26, so the explicit FAIL condition is not triggered.

expectations.text: Names at least one further concrete delta: e.g., measures ISO doesn't universally mandate (MFA under Art. 21(2)(j), cryptography policy under 21(2)(h)), sector-specific rules (Implementing Regulation 2024/2690), registration duties, or penalty exposure (Art. 34: up to €10M/2% for essential, €7M/1.4% for important entities)
expectations.evidence: 'Regulatory registration and scoping. NIS2 requires entities to determine whether they are "essential" or "important," register with the relevant NCA...' and 'NIS2 fines can reach up to €10 million or 2% of global annual turnover for essential entities (whichever is higher).' Also notes MFA as an explicit technical measure not always mandated by ISO SoA exclusions.
